metadata.title: Cost-of-virtue: silent extra share
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Locate the break-point stake at which the user takes more than their fair share of a group resource when they could do so undetected. This is essentially a one-shot dictator game with the recipient being the group rather than a single party, and with the user's stated value of cooperation/fairness explicitly in scope.
metadata.domain_anchor: Dictator/ultimatum literature (Forsythe 1994; Engel 2011 meta). Public-goods game literature. Galizzi & Navarro-Martinez 2019 lab-to-field meta on cooperation (mean r = 0.14 — individual-level signal is weaker than honesty-stakes paradigms; flagged as a probe whose external validity should be empirically demonstrated, not assumed).

framing_prompt: You and a small group did a project together — could be at work, a side gig, a volunteer thing. The group is now splitting a windfall (a bonus, a tip pool, a thank-you check from a client) and you're the one handling the math. No one will check your work. You could pay yourself more than your equal share.
framing_question: What's the smallest amount of 'extra' at which you'd take it?

no_option.text: I wouldn't take extra at any of these amounts.

analysis.interpretation_note: Group size is deliberately unspecified — a $1,000 extra share from a 2-person windfall is different from a 20-person windfall. The probe captures the threshold for *the action of skimming* rather than its scale. If group-size sensitivity becomes the design question, a counterbalanced variant (cov-reciprocity-002) should vary group size while holding ladder constant.
analysis.longitudinal_signal: Within-user trajectory tracks cooperation-under-stakes drift. Cooperation break-point may correlate weakly with trust break-point on a separate probe; the construct space is empirically open.
